item.title: Swiss stops in-flight duty-free sales
item.description: From 30 September 2026, duty-free products will only be available online. Up to 50% off from June.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Duty-free sales on board ending on September 30, 2026
- Products available only online on Worldshop
- Up to 50% discounts from June

## Key facts
- **What**: End of duty-free sales on board
- When: September 30, 2026
- Where: Swiss flights
- Who: Swiss International Air Lines
- Amount: Up to 50% discounts

Shortly before the end of the flight, the cabin crew used to ask passengers if they wanted to buy cigarettes, watches, jewelry, or perfumes. Well, soon Swiss will put an end to this traditional on-board ritual. The airline announced in a press release that duty-free sales on flights will cease at the end of September. From that moment on, duty-free products will be available exclusively in the 'Worldshop' online store of the Miles&More program.

Swiss is adapting to the changing purchasing habits of passengers. An analysis of sales on board its aircraft over the past few years has shown a clear result: fewer and fewer passengers make purchases on the plane. Impulse buying from the duty-free cart has become an exception. On the contrary, an increasing number of passengers inform themselves about the products once at home, compare prices online, and make purchases even before arriving at the airport.

### What changes for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers who use Swiss flights for their trips between Italy and Switzerland will have to adapt to this new way of shopping. Those who were used to making duty-free purchases during the flight will now have to plan their purchases online before departure. This could influence the choice of some cross-border workers who prefer to shop during the flight to avoid carrying bulky items with them.

### Discounts and sales

Before the end of duty-free product sales on board, there will be a sale. Starting in June, Swiss will offer a selection of remaining duty-free items at reduced prices, with discounts starting at 25% and up to stock exhaustion. This does not mean that we will no longer be able to buy anything on Swiss flights. Beverages, snacks, and other items will still be available.

### Practical implications

For cross-border workers who use Swiss flights for their trips, this decision could have a significant impact. Those who were used to making duty-free purchases during the flight will now have to plan their purchases online before departure. This could influence the choice of some cross-border workers who prefer to shop during the flight to avoid carrying bulky items with them.

Furthermore, the possibility of buying duty-free products online could offer greater convenience and a wider range of products compared to what is available on board. However, the lack of impulse purchases during the flight could reduce purchasing opportunities for some passengers. ### Analysis of Shopping Habits

The analysis of onboard sales has revealed that fewer and fewer passengers make purchases during the flight. This change is attributed to the growing trend of researching products online and comparing prices before making a purchase. Passengers now prefer to shop online, where they can find a wider range of products and often at more competitive prices.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before this decision, passengers could purchase a variety of duty-free products onboard, including cigarettes, watches, jewelry, and perfumes. This offered the opportunity for last-minute shopping and to take advantage of special offers available only onboard. With the end of duty-free sales onboard, passengers will now have to plan their purchases in advance and rely on the online store Worldshop.

### Future Scenarios

With the elimination of duty-free sales onboard, Swiss aims to optimize onboard operations and meet the needs of passengers who prefer to shop online. This change could also influence the marketing strategies of airlines, which might focus more on online sales and loyalty programs such as Miles&More.

### Impact on Cross-Border Commuters

For cross-border commuters who frequently travel between Italy and Switzerland, this decision could have a significant impact. Those who were used to making duty-free purchases during the flight will now have to plan their online purchases before departure. This could influence the choice of some cross-border commuters who prefer to shop during the flight to avoid carrying bulky items with them.

### Comparison with Other Airlines

Other airlines might follow Swiss's example and reduce or eliminate duty-free sales onboard. This could lead to greater standardization of sales practices among airlines and a greater emphasis on online sales. However, some airlines might choose to maintain duty-free sales onboard to attract passengers who prefer to shop during the flight.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do now

For passengers who wish to purchase duty-free products, it is advisable to start planning their purchases online before departure. The Worldshop online store offers a wide range of products and often features special offers and discounts. Additionally, passengers can take advantage of the upcoming sale starting in June, with discounts of up to 50% on a selection of remaining items.

### Online shopping procedure

1. Visit the Worldshop website of the Miles&More program.
2. Create an account or log in to your existing account.
3. Browse through the different categories of available products.
4. Add the desired products to your cart.
5. Complete the purchase by following the instructions on the site.
6. Receive the purchased products at the specified address or pick them up at a convenient pickup point.

### Using the Frontaliere Ticino calculator

To best plan their purchases and compare prices, cross-border workers can use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator. This tool allows you to compare the prices of duty-free products available online and calculate the savings compared to purchases made at the airport or on board.

### Conclusion

The decision by Swiss to discontinue duty-free sales on board is an adaptation to the new purchasing habits of passengers. Although this change may have a significant impact on some cross-border workers, it also offers the opportunity to explore new shopping methods and take advantage of the offers available online. For more information and to plan their purchases, cross-border workers can rely on the Worldshop online store and the tools provided by Frontaliere Ticino.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/svizzera/attualita/1921767/duty-free-prodotti-bordo-swiss-online)*

item.title: Company carpooling: cross-border workers earn up to 500 francs
item.description: The MomòRide project offers cash incentives and charitable opportunities for cross-border workers in Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Carpooling for cross-border workers with rewards up to 500 francs
- Collective goal of 40,000 points to donate 1,000 francs to the Fondazione Provvida Madre
- Participation open to workers and companies in Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano

## Key facts
- **What**: MomòRide corporate carpooling project
- When: Starting May 1, 2026
- Where: Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano
- Who: Cross-border workers and local companies
- Amount: Up to 500 francs in monthly incentives
- Goal: 40,000 points to donate 1,000 francs
- Sponsor: Banca dello Stato del Canton Ticino

On May 1, 2026, the first collective challenge MomòRide will begin, an initiative of corporate carpooling aimed at cross-border workers in Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano. This two-year project, promoted by the local municipalities, offers economic incentives and the opportunity to contribute to charitable initiatives. Participants can earn up to 500 francs per month by tracking their commutes with the Mobalt app. Additionally, by joining forces to reach the goal of 40,000 points in a month, the teams can donate 1,000 francs to the Fondazione Provvida Madre in Balerna.

### How it works
To participate, cross-border workers must track their carpooling trips with the Mobalt app. Each trip accumulates points that add up to those of other carpoolers in the area. Upon reaching the target of 40,000 points, Banca dello Stato del Canton Ticino, the sponsor of the challenge, will make the donation. This collective challenge model aims to create a virtuous circle, promoting carpooling and generating positive impacts for the environment and the community.

### Incentives and participation
With April, the fourth month of distributing the prize pool of 500 francs for teams from companies based in the three municipalities comes to a close. The number of participants is continuously growing, with new companies launching the project among their employees. The invitation to participate is extended to all workers in the three municipalities, offering not only individual monthly prizes but also the opportunity to concretely contribute to local entities that help those in need.

### Future prospects
Further collective challenges will be launched in the autumn, with other public utility entities in the area as recipients. This project represents an important opportunity for cross-border workers in Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano to benefit from economic incentives and actively contribute to the common good. ### Practical Implications for Cross-Border Workers
The MomòRide project offers numerous practical advantages for cross-border workers employed in the Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano area. Firstly, corporate carpooling helps reduce transportation costs by sharing fuel and toll expenses. Moreover, the opportunity to earn up to 500 francs per month serves as a significant economic incentive, which can be used to cover other daily expenses.

### Before/After Comparisons
Before the introduction of the MomòRide project, cross-border workers had to bear the costs of individual transportation, often high due to traffic and tolls. With corporate carpooling, not only are these costs reduced, but there is also the opportunity to earn monthly bonuses. Furthermore, the possibility of contributing to charitable initiatives adds a social value to the project, making it even more appealing.

### Concrete Scenarios
Imagine a cross-border worker who travels 50 kilometers daily to reach their workplace. With carpooling, they can share the journey with three other colleagues, thus reducing transportation costs by 75%. Additionally, by tracking their movements with the Mobalt app, they can accumulate points and earn up to 500 francs per month. If all participants join forces to reach the goal of 40,000 points, they can donate 1,000 francs to the Provvida Madre Foundation, actively contributing to the common good.

### Environmental Benefits
Carpooling not only offers economic advantages but also has a positive impact on the environment. By reducing the number of vehicles on the road, CO2 emissions are decreased, and the sustainability of the area is enhanced. This is particularly important in an area like Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano, where traffic is one of the main sources of pollution.

### Corporate Participation
Local companies are encouraged to promote the MomòRide project among their employees. By offering economic incentives and the opportunity to contribute to charitable initiatives, companies can improve the well-being of their employees and actively contribute to the common good. Furthermore, corporate carpooling can enhance productivity by reducing traffic-related stress and improving the quality of life for employees.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### How to Participate
To participate in the MomòRide project, cross-border workers must follow a few simple steps:
1. Download the Mobalt app on your smartphone.
2. Register as a user and select the carpooling option.
3. Track each commute shared by car with the app.
4. Earn points for every trip made.
5. Join other carpoolers in your area to reach the goal of 40,000 points.
6. Benefit from monthly prizes and contribute to charitable initiatives.

### Deadlines and Prizes
Monthly prizes of 500 francs are distributed to participants who track their trips with the Mobalt app. Additionally, each month a new collective challenge is launched with the goal of accumulating 40,000 points. Upon reaching this goal, the Bank of the Canton of Ticino donates 1,000 francs to a public utility entity in the territory.

### Useful Tools
To learn more about the opportunities offered by the MomòRide project and calculate potential savings and earnings, cross-border workers can use the [carpooling savings calculator](nav:calculator). This tool allows you to estimate individual transportation costs and potential savings from corporate carpooling.

### Conclusion
The MomòRide project represents an important opportunity for cross-border workers from Balerna, Chiasso, and Novazzano to benefit from economic incentives, contribute to the common good, and improve their quality of life. By participating in corporate carpooling, cross-border workers can reduce transportation costs, earn monthly prizes, and contribute to charitable initiatives. Don't miss the chance to join this initiative and make a difference in your community.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/frontalieri-con-il-carpooling-aziendale-ora-gli-equipaggi-guadagnano-fino-a-500-franchi-e-fanno-del-bene/)*

item.title: Swiss to end duty-free sales from September 30, 2026
item.description: Swiss airline will discontinue its in-flight duty-free sales service starting September 30, 2026.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Swiss to remove duty-free onboard from September 30, 2026
- Offer will remain available online via Miles & More
- Decision follows analysis revealing a decrease in inflight purchases

## Key facts
- **What**: Removal of onboard duty-free service
- When: From September 30, 2026
- Where: On all Swiss flights
- Who: Swiss International Air Lines
- Amount: Not specified

Swiss International Air Lines has announced that it will remove the onboard duty-free sales service starting from September 30, 2026. The decision was made following an analysis that revealed a decrease in inflight purchases. The current onboard offer includes items such as watches, jewelry, perfumes, cosmetic products, and various accessories. These products will still be available online on the Miles & More portal.

The airline is following in the footsteps of its parent company Lufthansa, which already removed inflight duty-free services last September. Swiss's decision was communicated through an official announcement released on Wednesday.

### Implications for passengers
Passengers who are used to shopping during Swiss flights will have to adapt to this new reality. The removal of onboard duty-free could influence the purchasing habits of frequent travelers with this airline. However, the option to buy duty-free products online via Miles & More remains a valid alternative.

### Comparison with Lufthansa
Lufthansa has already implemented this decision a year ago, and now Swiss is following the same path. This indicates a trend in the aviation sector towards reducing onboard services, likely due to decreased demand. Swiss's decision could be a signal for other airlines to follow the same example.

### What to expect in the future
With the removal of onboard duty-free, passengers might need to plan their purchases in advance. The option to buy online could become the norm for those wishing to purchase duty-free products. Additionally, this decision could influence the marketing strategies of airlines, which might focus more on digital and online services. - ## Analysis of the implications
The elimination of duty-free on board could have several implications for passengers and the aviation sector in general. First, passengers who are used to shopping during flights will have to adapt to this new reality. This could mean that they will have to plan their purchases in advance or look for alternatives to buy duty-free products.

### Impact on passengers
For passengers who frequently travel with Swiss, the elimination of duty-free on board could represent a significant change. Many passengers may have developed the habit of purchasing duty-free products during flights, especially for items such as perfumes, cosmetics, and accessories. With the elimination of this service, these passengers will have to find alternatives to meet their purchasing needs.

### Opportunities for the online market
Swiss's announcement could represent an opportunity for the online market. With the elimination of duty-free on board, passengers may be encouraged to shop online through the Miles & More portal. This could lead to an increase in online sales and greater competition among e-commerce platforms.

### Comparison with other airlines
Swiss's decision follows the example of Lufthansa, which has already eliminated duty-free on board. This could indicate a trend in the aviation sector towards the reduction of on-board services. Other airlines may follow the same example, especially if the analysis reveals a decrease in demand for duty-free services.

### Marketing strategies
The elimination of duty-free on board could influence the marketing strategies of airlines. Swiss could focus more on digital and online services, such as the Miles & More portal, to attract passengers and encourage online purchases. This could lead to greater integration between on-board services and digital platforms.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do now
With the elimination of duty-free on board, Swiss passengers will need to adapt to this new reality. Here are some steps they can follow to continue enjoying the benefits of duty-free shopping:

1. **Plan purchases in advance**: Passengers can plan their purchases further in advance and use the Miles & More portal to buy the desired products.
2. Explore online options: The Miles & More portal offers a wide range of duty-free products that can be purchased online before the flight.
3. Compare prices: Passengers can compare the prices of products online with those available at the airport or in local stores to find the best deals.
4. Use the benefits of the loyalty program: Members of the Miles & More program can use their points to purchase duty-free products online, thus obtaining additional benefits.

### Useful tools
To help passengers plan their purchases, Swiss has made a series of online tools available. These tools can be used to compare prices, explore purchasing options, and plan purchases in advance. Additionally, passengers can use the Miles & More portal to purchase the desired products and take advantage of the benefits of the loyalty program.

### Conclusion
The elimination of duty-free on board by Swiss represents a significant change for passengers. However, with the right planning and use of online tools, passengers can continue to enjoy the benefits of duty-free shopping. Use the [spending calculator](nav:cost-of-living) to plan your purchases and compare the prices of products available online.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3702873)*

item.title: Swiss fuel reserves secured until May 2026
item.description: Swiss fuel stocks are exclusively for national needs. We explore the current situation and implications for cross-border workers.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Swiss fuel reserves guaranteed until May 2026
- Reserves exclusively for national needs
- Energy solidarity agreement for gas with Italy and Germany

## Key facts
- **What**: Mandatory fuel reserves
- When: Guaranteed until the end of May 2026
- Where: Swiss depots, such as Mellingen (Aargau)
- Who: Federal Office for National Economic Supply (FONES)
- Amount: Autonomy of approximately 72 days for jet fuel

Despite the war with Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, diesel and gasoline continue to flow to service stations in Switzerland and planes continue to take off. However, the situation is tense regarding jet fuel. The supply of Swiss airports is guaranteed until the end of May 2026, according to the Federal Office for National Economic Supply (FONES).

In case of shortages, the Confederation can authorize the withdrawal from mandatory reserves. This applies to all fuels and fuels. Unlike the countries of the European Union (EU), Swiss reserves are exclusively for national needs. If jet fuel were to become scarce in an EU state, the other member countries would have to apply the so-called principle of energy solidarity. Germany, for example, would be obliged to assist another Union country by providing its own fuel.

### Energy solidarity and international agreements
Swiss reserves, on the other hand, are exclusively for Switzerland, says Nicole Mathys, Head of Energy at FONES. 'Switzerland is not a member of the EU and is therefore not bound by the principle of energy solidarity,' she emphasizes. However, there is an exception. Regarding gas supply, there is an agreement between Switzerland, Italy and Germany, Mathys specifies. If gas were to become scarce in one of these countries, it would be possible to request reciprocal solidarity measures. 'In this case, the focus is on the supply of gas to protected customers, such as households, hospitals or emergency services,' she explains. These customers would therefore have priority for supplies.

### Priorities and open market
Unlike gas, there is no form of priority for fuels. The fact that Switzerland does not provide for an energy solidarity mechanism does not mean that it could not be affected by a possible fuel shortage. Given that in case of shortages, fuels such as jet fuel, but also diesel or gasoline, would be put on the market from the Swiss mandatory reserves through traditional channels, it is possible that foreign traders buy fuel, explains Mathys: 'Theoretically, someone can buy it and resell it on the other side of the border.' The market, therefore, would remain open.

### Exceptions and priorities
The Federal Council can also decide on exceptions. 'If, for example, jet fuel were to become extremely scarce, the Federal Council could order that, for example, rescue, firefighting and search flights receive the jet fuel supplies they need,' explains Mathys. At the moment, however, a similar eventuality still seems far off. All these rules, however, do not concern the army. According to FONES, the armed forces have their own jet fuel reserves. Their fuel is therefore not stored with the mandatory reserves that the Confederation manages in collaboration with the Swiss petroleum industry.

### Reserves lower than expected
Mandatory reserves are intended to cope with situations such as the current one, but the jet fuel reserves are lower than expected according to the ordinance in force, which for aviation fuel establishes a coverage of three months. Currently, according to FONES, the autonomy is approximately 72 days. The discrepancy is explained by the method of calculating the reserves: the current value of the quantity to be stored is based on the average fuel consumption from 2022 to 2024. From the end of 2024 to the outbreak of the war in Iran, however, consumption has increased significantly, and it is therefore necessary to increase the reserves, in addition to guaranteeing the normal supply of fuel. - ### Implications for cross-border workers
The Swiss fuel reserves, intended exclusively for national needs, could have a significant impact on cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy. In case of shortages, fuels such as kerosene, diesel, and gasoline would be released onto the market from the Swiss mandatory reserves through traditional channels. This could lead to an increase in prices or greater difficulty in obtaining fuel, especially in the event of an international crisis.

### Crisis scenarios
If the situation were to worsen, the Federal Council could decide to impose restrictions on fuel use. For example, rescue, firefighting, and search flights could have priority for kerosene supplies. This could affect air travel and freight transport, with possible repercussions on the work of cross-border workers who depend on these services.

### Open market and foreign traders
The Swiss fuel market remains open, meaning that foreign traders could buy Swiss fuel and resell it in other countries. This could lead to an increase in prices in Switzerland, but also to a greater availability of fuel in Italy, indirectly benefiting cross-border workers.

### Energy solidarity agreements
The energy solidarity agreement between Switzerland, Italy, and Germany for gas represents an example of international collaboration. In case of gas shortages, the three countries can request reciprocal solidarity measures, with priority for protected customers such as households, hospitals, and emergency services. This agreement could be a model for future collaborations in the field of energy supply.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to do in case of fuel shortages
In case of fuel shortages, it's important to be prepared and informed. Here are some steps to follow:

1. **Monitor the news**: Stay updated on the latest news regarding fuel supply in Switzerland and Italy.
2. Plan your trips: In case of restrictions, plan your trips in advance and consider alternatives like public transport or carpooling.
3. Reduce consumption: Adopt measures to reduce fuel consumption, such as driving more efficiently or using electric vehicles.
4. Use the fuel cost calculator: To compare fuel prices in Switzerland and Italy, use the [fuel cost calculator](nav:calculator).
5. Consult practical guides: For more information on how to manage fuel shortages, consult the practical guides available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch) website.

### Conclusions
Swiss fuel reserves guarantee supply until the end of May 2026. However, it's important to be prepared and informed in case of a crisis. Cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy should monitor the situation and adopt measures to reduce fuel consumption. Using tools like the [fuel cost calculator](nav:calculator) and consulting practical guides can help better manage any fuel shortages.

*Source: [rsi.ch](https://www.rsi.ch/s/3699156)*

item.title: AHV/IV: digital data available from 2028
item.description: The National Council has approved the new law on information systems for social insurances. Savings of 35 million francs
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- AVS/AI data digitally consultable from 2028
- Savings of 35 million francs per year
- Optional platform for insured individuals

## Key facts
- **What**: New law on information systems of social insurances
- When: Approved on April 29, 2026, expected to come into force in 2028
- Where: Switzerland, with impact on cross-border workers in Ticino
- Who: National Council, Federal Council, Central Compensation Office
- Amount: Savings of 35 million francs per year, including 25 million in postal expenses

The National Council has approved the new law on information systems of social insurances, which will allow insured individuals to consult their AVS and AI files in digital format. The project, supported by the parliamentary groups of the PS, the Centre, the PLR, the Greens and the Green Liberals, was approved with 131 votes in favor and 62 against. The UDC expressed its dissent, fearing excessive centralization. The dossier will now pass to the Council of States.

Currently, insured individuals who wish to obtain information about their first pillar must wait several weeks for a response, as data exchange is not automated and often relies on PDF or paper documents. The new digital platform will allow significant savings, estimated at 35 million francs per year, including 25 million in postal expenses.

### Implications for cross-border workers
Insured individuals will be able to use the platform to provisionally calculate their AVS pension, check for any contribution gaps or verify if employers have correctly paid contributions. The use of the platform will remain optional and will require authentication through an electronic identity recognized by the Government. Executive bodies and insurers will, however, have a legal obligation to communicate with each other electronically.

The National Council made some changes to the government's draft to best manage the digital transformation of the first pillar. The Central Compensation Office will have to involve the executive bodies in the development and management of the new AVS/AI and family allowance platform. These actors will have to set up a strategic steering body, responsible for defining priorities, implementation methods and standards for information systems at national level.

### Procedure and deadlines
The new platform will be available from 2028. Insured individuals who wish to use the platform will have to register and authenticate themselves through an electronic identity recognized. Registration will be optional, but once completed, users will automatically receive an extract of the AVS contributions paid each year, allowing them to report any contribution gaps.

For cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy, this new platform represents a significant step towards greater transparency and efficiency in the management of AVS contributions. The possibility of consulting their data in digital format and receiving annual extracts of the contributions paid will facilitate pension planning and verification of the correctness of payments. ### Advantages of Digitalization
The digitalization of AVS/AI data will bring numerous benefits for cross-border workers and insured individuals in general. Among the main advantages are:

- **Reduced waiting times**: Currently, obtaining information about one's first pillar can take several weeks. With the new platform, waiting times will be significantly reduced, allowing insured individuals to quickly access their data.
- Greater transparency: The ability to consult one's files in digital format and receive annual extracts of the contributions paid will increase transparency and allow insured individuals to more easily monitor their contributions.
- Economic savings: Digitalization will lead to estimated savings of 35 million francs per year, of which 25 million in postal expenses. These savings can be reinvested in other sectors of the pension system.

### Comparison with the Current Situation
Currently, the exchange of data related to social insurance is not automated and often relies on documents in PDF or paper format. This process is not only slow but also prone to errors and inefficiencies. The new digital platform will allow the standardization of electronic data exchange, reducing the risk of errors and improving the overall efficiency of the system.

### Practical Scenarios
Imagine a cross-border worker who works in Switzerland and resides in Italy. With the new digital platform, this cross-border worker will be able to:

- Calculate their AVS pension: Using the platform, the cross-border worker can provisionally calculate their AVS pension, facilitating financial planning for retirement.
- Verify contributions paid: The cross-border worker can check if their AVS contributions have been correctly paid by their employers, avoiding any contribution gaps.
- Receive annual extracts: Each year, the cross-border worker will automatically receive an extract of the AVS contributions paid, allowing them to constantly monitor their pension situation.

### Implications for Employers
Employers in Switzerland will be required to communicate electronically with the executive bodies and insurers. This legal obligation will ensure that AVS contributions are correctly and timely paid, reducing the risk of errors and omissions. Employers will also need to ensure that their employees are informed about the new platform and the methods of accessing their data.

### Involvement of Executive Bodies
The Central Compensation Office will need to involve the executive bodies in the development and management of the new platform. This strategic, technical, and operational involvement will ensure that the platform meets the needs of all parties involved and is implemented efficiently and securely. The executive bodies will also need to establish a strategic steering body, tasked with defining priorities, implementation methods, and standards for information systems at the national level.

## Useful planning tools
To estimate your pension strategy, use the [pension planner](nav:pension) and the [pillar 3 simulator](nav:pillar3). ### What to do to access the platform
To access the new AVS/AI digital platform, insured individuals will need to follow these steps:

1. **Registration**: Register on the platform using an electronic identity recognized by the Government.
2. Authentication: Log in to the platform using the credentials provided during registration.
3. Data consultation: Access your AVS and AI files and consult the available data.
4. Pension calculation: Use the tools available on the platform to provisionally calculate your AVS pension.
5. Contribution verification: Check if AVS contributions have been correctly paid by employers and report any contribution gaps.
6. Receiving annual statements: Automatically receive an annual statement of AVS contributions paid and constantly monitor your pension situation.

### Useful tools
To facilitate access to the new platform and the management of your AVS/AI data, insured individuals can use the following tools:

- AVS pension calculator: An online tool that allows you to provisionally calculate your AVS pension.
- Contribution verification: A tool that allows you to check if AVS contributions have been correctly paid by employers.
- Annual statements: A tool that allows you to automatically receive an annual statement of AVS contributions paid.

### Conclusion
The new law on information systems for social insurances represents a significant step towards greater transparency and efficiency in the management of AVS/AI contributions. For cross-border workers who work in Switzerland and reside in Italy, this new digital platform will offer numerous advantages, including reduced waiting times, greater transparency, and the ability to constantly monitor your pension situation. To learn more, consult the [AVS pension calculator](nav:calculator).

item.title: Switzerland increasingly cashless, SNB seeks fairer system
item.description: Cash payments drop to 30% of transactions, payment apps reach 20%. The SNB warns about concentration risks.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Cash is used in 30% of transactions at the till
- Payment apps account for 20%
- The SNB warns about the risks of market concentration

## Key facts
- **What**: Use of cash and payment apps
- When: 2024 data
- Where: Switzerland
- Who: Swiss National Bank (SNB)
- Amount: 30% cash, 20% payment apps

Cashless payments continue to gain ground in Switzerland. According to the periodic surveys by the Swiss National Bank (SNB), cash is now used in approximately 30% of transactions at the till, while payment apps – with Twint leading the way – have reached 20% of operations. This scenario was highlighted by Petra Tschudin, a member of the SNB's Executive Board, in a recent speech in Zurich, where she also issued a clear warning: market concentration in digital payments is a real risk and requires targeted interventions.

### The three speeds of payments in Switzerland

The retail payment landscape has transformed rapidly over the past decade. The 2024 SNB survey – the most recent with complete data – provides a precise picture: debit cards are the most used means, accounting for 35% of purchases; cash holds 30% of in-person transactions; payment apps reach 20% and credit cards the remaining 14%. For comparison, in 2017 cash was still at 70%: a shift of forty points in seven years, unprecedented in the history of Swiss payments.

### The risk of concentration

It is precisely on apps and payment networks that the most interesting game is being played. According to the SNB, the Swiss market is currently dominated by four major players: Mastercard, Visa, PostFinance, and Twint. A concentration that carries with it the risk of an oligopolistic drift, with effects on competition, commissions, and ultimately on costs for merchants and consumers.

### The SNB's intervention

Faced with this scenario, Tschudin outlined a distinction worth remembering. Not all segments of the payment system require the same level of competition. For the central settlement infrastructure – the Swiss Interbank Clearing (SIC) system, managed by SIX on behalf of the SNB – there is a 'natural monopoly': having a single operator is the most efficient solution, and the SNB ensures that this position is not abused. Different is the case for payment networks. Here, Tschudin explained, 'neither a monopoly nor a market with many competitors is desirable': a balanced mix of competition is needed.

### Concrete measures

To counter the trend towards concentration, the SNB proposes concrete measures: regulation of interchange fees, reduction of entry barriers, and forced opening of technological interfaces. One of the hottest fronts concerns the so-called interchange fees, the commissions that networks charge merchants for each transaction. In Switzerland, the Competition Commission (COMCO) has already launched an investigation into this issue, which could lead to significant changes in the payment market. ### Implications for cross-border workers

The transition towards an increasingly digital payment system has significant implications for cross-border workers employed in Switzerland. With the rise in the use of payment apps and debit cards, it is crucial for cross-border workers to be aware of the available options and associated costs. For instance, using Twint or other payment apps can offer advantages in terms of convenience and speed, but it is important to compare the fees and conditions offered by different providers.

### Comparison of payment methods

To help cross-border workers navigate this landscape, it is useful to compare the different payment methods available. Here is a comparative table showing the pros and cons of each method:

| Payment Method | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Cash | Anonymity, no fees | Less convenient, risk of theft |
| Debit Card | Convenient, widely accepted | Possible fees, risk of fraud |
| Payment App | Speed, convenience | Dependence on technology, fees |
| Credit Card | Rewards, consumer protection | High fees, risk of debt |

### Future scenarios

With the evolution of the payment system, various future scenarios can be envisioned. An optimistic scenario sees a more equitable and competitive payment market, with greater choice for consumers and lower costs for merchants. However, a pessimistic scenario could lead to increased market concentration, with a few dominant operators dictating the terms.

### What to do now

For cross-border workers, it is important to keep up with changes and adopt new payment technologies. It is advisable to explore the different options available and choose the one that best suits their needs. Additionally, it is crucial to monitor any regulatory changes and decisions by the Swiss National Bank (BNS) that could influence the payment market. ### Concrete steps for cross-border workers

1. **Evaluate payment options**: Compare fees and conditions offered by different payment service providers.
2. Adopt new technologies: Experiment with payment apps like Twint and other digital platforms.
3. Monitor regulations: Stay updated on BNS decisions and any regulatory changes.
4. Compare costs: Use comparison tools to assess the costs associated with different payment methods.
5. Protect personal data: Ensure the use of secure payment platforms and protect personal information.

### Useful tools

To help cross-border workers navigate the complex world of digital payments, Frontaliere Ticino offers a series of useful tools. Among these, the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) can help compare the costs associated with different payment methods and plan expenses. Additionally, the [digital payments guide](nav:exchange) provides detailed information on the available options and the steps to follow to make the best use of new technologies.

### Conclusion

The transition to an increasingly digital payment system offers both opportunities and challenges for cross-border workers. With the right information and adequate tools, it is possible to successfully navigate this new landscape and take advantage of new technologies. Staying informed and adopting a proactive approach are fundamental to addressing the ongoing changes.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/newsblog/valute-e-frontiere/1918997/svizzera-sempre-piu-cashless-ma-la-bns-vuole-un-sistema-piu-equo)*

item.title: Von der Leyen: Iran war costs EU 500 million daily for energy
item.description: The European Commission president warns that the Middle East conflict is causing severe economic repercussions.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Iran war costs EU 500 million a day for energy
- Conflict represents the second major energy crisis in 4 years
- Von der Leyen: 'We must reduce dependence on imported fossil fuels'

## Key facts
- **What**: Conflict in Iran
- When: For 2 months
- Where: Middle East
- Who: European Commission
- Amount: 27 billion euros in 60 days

The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, stated that the war in Iran is costing the European Union 500 million euros a day for energy. In her speech to the European Parliament, two months after the start of the conflict, von der Leyen emphasized that the consequences could be felt for months or even years to come. The conflict represents the 'second major energy crisis in the short span of four years,' the EU executive leader said.

Von der Leyen reiterated the EU's commitment to ending the war, fully reopening the Strait of Hormuz, and a peace agreement that includes Iranian nuclear power. 'The lesson should be clear for everyone: in a turbulent world like ours, we cannot simply be overly dependent on imported energy,' she declared. In the first 60 days of the conflict, the EU has spent 27 billion euros more on imports of fossil fuels.

### Implications for cross-border workers

Von der Leyen's statements have important implications for cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Italy. The EU's energy dependence could influence energy costs and, consequently, the cost of living. Cross-border workers should closely monitor the evolution of the situation and consider measures to reduce their own energy consumption, such as installing solar panels or adopting low-consumption appliances.

### Measures to reduce energy dependence

Von der Leyen emphasized that member countries with a higher share of low-emission sources in their energy mix are less affected by the crisis. In a country like Sweden, when the price of gas increases by 1 euro per megawatt-hour (MWh), the electricity bill increases by only 0.04 euros per MWh, because almost all Swedish electricity comes from renewables and nuclear power. 'This is how we insulate ourselves from future shocks, and this is the path to an independent Europe,' she noted.

The leader of the European Commission acknowledged that each EU country has a different energy mix, which is why the series of measures presented last week 'can be combined differently in the various parts of the Union.' These are actions to be taken in three areas: greater coordination at the European level, protection of consumers and businesses, and reduction of energy demand.

### Concrete actions for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers can adopt various measures to reduce their energy dependence and save money. For example, they can install solar panels, adopt low-consumption appliances, and use more efficient heating systems. Additionally, they can closely monitor their energy consumption and adopt more sustainable behaviors, such as turning off lights when not needed and using appliances only when they are full.

For more information on how to reduce energy consumption and save money, cross-border workers can consult the [energy cost calculator](nav:calculator). - ## Analysis of the proposed measures

Von der Leyen has presented a series of measures to reduce the EU's energy dependence. These measures include greater European coordination, protection for consumers and businesses, and a reduction in energy demand. Greater European coordination involves filling national gas storage facilities and fuel reserves, particularly for aircraft and diesel. 'We can be much more effective if we coordinate the release of oil reserves and ensure that refineries can increase production across our entire Union,' she stated.

### Protection for consumers and businesses

The second area of intervention concerns protecting consumers and businesses with measures 'exclusively targeted at the most vulnerable families and industries, avoiding an increase in gas and oil demand.' During the last crisis, only a quarter of the emergency support was targeted in this way, and over 350 billion euros were spent on non-targeted measures, with an 'enormous impact on the finances of member states' and undermining protection for those who needed it most. 'Let's not repeat the same mistake and concentrate our support where it counts most,' von der Leyen emphasized.

### Reducing energy demand

Thirdly, von der Leyen stated that the EU must reduce energy demand by modernizing systemic energy use. In the short term, this can be achieved through a combination of energy efficiency, electrification, and faster implementation of digital technologies. However, she emphasized that, overall, we will need abundant energy, especially due to the rapid expansion of data centers and artificial intelligence.

### Implications for cross-border workers

The measures proposed by von der Leyen could have significant implications for cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Italy. For example, greater European coordination could lead to greater stability in energy prices, while protecting consumers and businesses could reduce the impact of the energy crisis on the cost of living. Furthermore, reducing energy demand could lead to greater sustainability and a lesser environmental impact.

### Concrete actions for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers can adopt various measures to reduce their energy dependence and save money. For example, they can install solar panels, adopt low-consumption appliances, and use more efficient heating systems. Additionally, they can carefully monitor energy consumption and adopt more sustainable behaviors, such as turning off lights when not necessary and using appliances only when they are full.

For further information on how to reduce energy consumption and save money, cross-border workers can consult the [energy cost calculator](nav:calculator).

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do now

Cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Italy should closely monitor the evolution of the energy situation and consider measures to reduce their energy consumption. For example, they can install solar panels, adopt low-consumption appliances, and use more efficient heating systems. Additionally, they can closely monitor energy consumption and adopt more sustainable behaviors, such as turning off lights when not needed and using appliances only when full.

### Concrete steps

1. **Evaluate energy consumption**: Cross-border workers should evaluate their energy consumption and identify areas where they can reduce usage.
2. Install solar panels: Installing solar panels can significantly reduce electricity consumption and save money.
3. Adopt low-consumption appliances: Purchasing low-consumption appliances can reduce energy consumption and save money.
4. Use more efficient heating systems: Using more efficient heating systems can reduce energy consumption and save money.
5. Monitor energy consumption: Cross-border workers should closely monitor energy consumption and adopt more sustainable behaviors, such as turning off lights when not needed and using appliances only when full.

### Useful tools

For more information on how to reduce energy consumption and save money, cross-border workers can consult the [energy cost calculator](nav:calculator). Additionally, they can consult the guides and tips available on the [Frontaliere Ticino](https://frontaliereticino.ch) website to obtain updated and useful information.

### Conclusion

The war in Iran is causing serious economic repercussions for the European Union, with a daily cost of 500 million euros for energy. Cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Italy should closely monitor the evolution of the situation and consider measures to reduce their energy consumption. By adopting more sustainable behaviors and using useful tools, cross-border workers can reduce their energy dependence and save money.

*Source: [varesenoi.it](https://www.varesenoi.it/2026/04/29/leggi-notizia/articolo/von-der-leyen-guerra-in-iran-costa-allue-500-milioni-al-giorno-per-lenergia.html)*

item.title: Secure energy supply in Switzerland
item.description: The attack on Iran and the retaliation have increased the prices of petrol and diesel in Switzerland too, but the supply is secure.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Middle East conflict blocks a fifth of global oil
- Diesel prices +8.6%, heating oil +40% in Switzerland
- Switzerland's supply secure, guaranteed

## Key facts
- **What**: Increase in oil and gas prices
- When: 28 February - 9 March 2026
- Where: Switzerland
- Who: Avenergy, Energy Supply Security Steering Committee
- Amount: Diesel +8.6%, heating oil +40%

The attack on Iran and its retaliation have caused an increase in gasoline and diesel prices in Switzerland as well. The conflict in the Middle East has disrupted global oil and gas transport. The consequences are also felt in Switzerland, although the country does not directly depend on the Persian Gulf for its energy supply. Here is where the fossil fuels consumed in Switzerland come from.

The price of oil – and even more so that of natural gas – has skyrocketed following the military offensive by Israel and the United States against Iran, and the subsequent retaliation by Tehran. Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, a small maritime corridor that separates the Arabian Peninsula from the Iranian coast. It threatens to strike ships attempting to cross it. This means that about a fifth of the world's crude oil trade is blocked, as ships carrying oil and gas produced in Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and other Middle Eastern countries cannot move.

### Supply stability

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has repercussions for all oil-consuming countries, including those – like Switzerland – that do not depend on crude oil passing through the strait. Even if oil producers were to quickly resume normal activities, the market will remain unstable for months, according to analysts.

This also applies to Switzerland. Diesel and heating oil prices have increased by 8.6% and 40% respectively between 28 February and 9 March. However, fossil fuel supplies have not been affected so far. Avenergy, the organisation that brings together the actors in the Swiss oil market, stated on 9 March that the country's oil supply is currently 'secure', as the market has sufficient quantities of oil.

### Official statements

'Our supply of petroleum products is always guaranteed; there is no fear of shortages,' says Ueli Bamert, spokesperson for Avenergy, to Swissinfo. Transport and logistics are very resilient and, if a source is unable to deliver, products can be imported from other regions, he emphasises.

On 17 March, the Energy Supply Security Steering Committee, which brings together political and business leaders as well as representatives of cantons and cities, also stated that there is no immediate risk to energy in Switzerland.

The stability of the supply is due to the fact that Switzerland sources its fossil fuels from countries outside the Middle East. However, even if supplies are assured, the country and its population will still feel repercussions on prices and the cost of living due to tensions in the region. These effects could intensify if the conflict were to drag on for several months. ### Implications for cross-border workers and residents in Ticino

The conflict in the Middle East and the rise in oil and gas prices have repercussions for cross-border workers and residents in Ticino. While supply is secure, energy costs could increase, affecting the cost of living.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before the conflict, oil and gas prices were stable, with a secure and constant supply. The current situation has led to an increase in prices, but not to shortages. This is a significant change compared to the stability of the previous months.

### Future scenarios

If the conflict were to persist, prices could remain high for months, further influencing the cost of living. However, Switzerland, thanks to its diversification of supply sources, is in a relatively secure position compared to other countries more dependent on Middle Eastern oil.

### Impact on transport and industry

Petroleum-derived products cover almost half of Switzerland's energy needs (46% in 2024). They are fundamental for transport, building heating, and industrial processes. An increase in prices could therefore have a significant impact on these sectors.

Natural gas, which accounts for about 12% of Swiss demand, is mainly used to heat homes and for cooking. In this case too, a price increase could influence the cost of living, especially for cross-border workers who live in Ticino and work in Italy, where energy costs could be higher.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What cross-border workers should do

Cross-border workers residing in Ticino and working in Italy should closely monitor the evolution of oil and gas prices. It is advisable to adopt measures to reduce energy consumption, such as using energy-efficient appliances and improving the thermal insulation of homes.

### Procedure to reduce energy costs

1. **Monitor consumption**: Use devices to monitor energy consumption and identify areas for improvement.
2. Reduce energy consumption: Turn off appliances when not in use, use LED bulbs, and adopt sustainable energy behaviors.
3. Insulate your home: Improve the thermal insulation of homes to reduce heat loss.
4. Use renewable energy sources: Consider installing solar panels or other renewable energy sources to reduce dependence on fossil fuels.

### Useful tools

For cross-border workers who want to calculate their energy consumption and find ways to reduce it, the [energy calculator](nav:calculator) available on the site can be used. This tool can help identify areas for improvement and plan interventions to reduce energy costs.

### Conclusion

Despite the rise in oil and gas prices, supply in Switzerland remains secure. However, it is important to adopt measures to reduce energy consumption and monitor price developments. Using tools like the energy calculator can help better manage costs and plan effective interventions.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/lavoro-ed-economia/petrolio-e-gas-in-svizzera-approvvigionamento-sicuro-nonostante-il-conflitto-in-medio-oriente/91143350)*

item.title: Greens praise Rapinese for tree care
item.description: Green Party leader Elisabetta Patelli praises Rapinese administration for tree care in Como, ahead of the no-confidence vote against the Green Councillor.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Elisabetta Patelli praises the Rapinese administration for tree care in Como.
- High-quality technical intervention on Viale Varese with four new trees.
- Tomorrow, the municipal council will discuss the motion of no confidence against the Green Spaces Councillor Chiara Bodero Maccabeo.

## Key facts
- **What**: Praise from Green Party leader Elisabetta Patelli to the Rapinese administration.
- When: 28 April 2026.
- Where: Viale Varese, Como.
- Who: Elisabetta Patelli, leader of the Como Greens and representative of Europa Verde.
- Amount: Four new trees planted (three maples and one birch).

The leader of the Como Greens, Elisabetta Patelli, expressed an unexpected praise for the administration of Mayor Alessandro Rapinese. In a note released in the last few hours, Patelli commended the way specialized technicians intervened on the trees in Viale Varese. This gesture of sportsmanship comes after months of controversy over other trees, such as the cherry trees. Tonight, the municipal council will also discuss the motion of no confidence against the Green Spaces Councillor Chiara Bodero Maccabeo, postponed from yesterday's assembly, 27 April. The motion was presented by the Pd, an ally of Alleanza Verdi e Sinistra, of which Europa Verde is a part.

### High-quality intervention on Viale Varese

Today in Viale Varese, Patelli witnessed an intervention on the tree heritage that can be defined as a model of good practice for the city. The planting of four new species – three maples and one birch – to replace missing or dead trees was not just a simple replacement, but a high-quality technical and agronomic intervention. The choice was made for specimens grown in pots, a more expensive option but definitely more reliable in terms of establishment and overall quality. Adequate manure mixed with soil was provided, fundamental to promote nutrition and growth in the first years. The anchoring was done with a hypogeal system using biodegradable wooden elements and wire, a solution that accompanies the plant in the initial phase and allows it, over time, to develop natural stability. The roots were left free to expand in the soil, avoiding harmful constraints.

A particularly relevant element concerns the quality of the trees: these are not young shrubs, but already formed plants, about eight years old, about six meters high, with structured and intact canopies. No forced pruning during transport – a practice unfortunately widespread that weakens the specimens – but an approach respectful of the plant's physiology. This is added to a guarantee of establishment of about two years, a further indicator of the seriousness of the intervention.

We are facing a naturalistic operation applied to the urban context, capable of combining aesthetics, functionality, and sustainability. This intervention demonstrates that another way of managing public green spaces is possible. It is exactly this direction that the Administration must take. We will ensure that it does not become, as has happened so far, an exception, but rather a consolidated practice. Because urban green spaces are vital infrastructure, public health, quality of life. Elisabetta Patelli's praise for the Rapinese administration is a gesture of great sportsmanship, especially in a tense political context. The no-confidence motion against Green Councilor Chiara Bodero Maccabeo, presented by the Pd, could put the administration in difficulty. However, Patelli's intervention could offer unexpected support to Mayor Rapinese, especially in view of tomorrow evening's debate. The intervention on Viale Varese is an example of how urban green management can be improved, with an approach that combines aesthetics, functionality, and sustainability.

### Implications for Urban Green Management

The intervention on Viale Varese demonstrates that urban green spaces can be managed in a more environmentally friendly and plant-respectful manner. The use of already formed trees, grown in pots with adequate manure, ensures better rooting and healthier growth. This approach could become a model for other cities, not just Como. The two-year rooting guarantee is a further indicator of the seriousness of the intervention and the willingness to ensure quality urban greenery.

### Comparison with the Previous Situation

Before this intervention, urban green management in Como had been the subject of controversy, especially regarding the cherry trees. The intervention on Viale Varese represents a significant change, with a more technical and plant-respectful approach. This could be a positive sign for the future management of urban greenery, not only in Como but also in other cities.

### Future Scenarios

If this approach becomes a consolidated practice, it could significantly improve the quality of urban greenery in Como. However, it is important that the administration continues to monitor that this is not an exception but a common practice. Urban green management is a matter of public health and quality of life, and as such must be treated with the utmost attention.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). For the citizens of Como, the intervention on Viale Varese represents an example of how urban green space management can be improved. If the administration continues on this path, it could significantly improve the quality of urban green spaces in the city. For cross-border workers who live and work in Como, this could mean a better quality of life and a healthier environment. The intervention on Viale Varese is a positive signal, but it is important that the administration continues to monitor it to ensure this approach becomes a consolidated practice.

### What to do

Citizens of Como can monitor urban green space management interventions and report any issues to the administration. They can also participate in municipal council discussions and push for the approach adopted on Viale Varese to become the norm. For cross-border workers, it is important to stay informed about the administration's decisions and actively participate in city life.

### Useful tools

To stay updated on the administration's decisions and actively participate in city life, citizens and cross-border workers can use the [tax calculator](nav:calculator) to better understand the impact of administrative decisions on their daily lives. They can also consult the [event calendar](nav:calendar) to attend municipal council meetings and other city initiatives.

### Final CTA

For more information and to stay updated on the administration's decisions, visit the [tax calculator](nav:calculator) and consult the [event calendar](nav:calendar).

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/ambiente/a-como-lelogio-che-non-ti-aspetti-la-leader-dei-verdi-allamministrazione-rapinese-bravi-e-cosi-che-si-curano-gli-alberi/)*

item.title: Blooming cities: balconies in Como and Cernobbio honor the Tricolor
item.description: Orticolario launches the 'Cities in Bloom' initiative to celebrate the 80th anniversary of the Italian Republic, transforming balconies and shop windows into Tricolor-themed artworks.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Orticolario launches 'Città in fiore' for the 80th anniversary of the Republic
- Balconies and shop windows in Como and Cernobbio will celebrate the Tricolore
- Participation open to residents, businesses, and schools

## Key facts
- **What**: 'Città in fiore' initiative to celebrate the 80th anniversary of the Italian Republic
- When: Deadline for applications May 24, 2026
- Where: Como and Cernobbio
- Who: Orticolario, Grandi Giardini Italiani, Fondazione Minoprio, Prefettura di Como
- Amount: Not specified

On the occasion of the eightieth anniversary of the Italian Republic, Orticolario has launched the 'Città in fiore' initiative, an invitation to transform the city into a dispersed garden. Shop windows, balconies, and windowsills overlooking the streets of Como and Cernobbio become places of creative expression and community value. The initiative is open to everyone: residents, businesses, schools, and associations. Each composition, simple or elaborate, will contribute to creating a great collective story spread through the streets, neighborhoods, and shores of the lake. Each participant is asked to interpret, with a creative eye, the Tricolore: green, white, and red. To participate, simply fill out an online form with the materials of the realized setup, by Sunday, May 24, 2026. A jury will select the most original balcony, which will be featured on Orticolario's and Grandi Giardini Italiani's official channels on Tuesday, June 2, Republic Day.

### Participation and Selection
To make the initiative even more alive and shared, the project also involves a selection of florists and local realities, daily custodians of beauty and expertise. Within their points of sale, corners dedicated to the Tricolore will take shape: compositions, plants, and materials designed to inspire, suggest pairings, and accompany citizens in creating their own setups. With this project, Orticolario renews its commitment to promoting green culture as a tool for connecting people, territories, and traditions.

### Objectives and Values
'Città in fiore' aims to be not only an aesthetic gesture but also a concrete sign of collective participation, capable of strengthening the sense of community and enhancing the local identity heritage. Como and Cernobbio transform into a shared garden, where green meets history and citizens become protagonists of a lively and participatory celebration. Full regulations and application methods on Orticolario's website. Initiative promoted by Orticolario, in collaboration with Grandi Giardini Italiani, Fondazione Minoprio, and Prefettura di Como, with the support of Confcommercio – Como and Confesercenti Como, and the patronage of the Municipality of Como and the Municipality of Cernobbio. Part of the 'Semm Italian' program. ### Implications for the Community
The 'Città in fiore' initiative not only celebrates an important anniversary but also strengthens the sense of community among the residents of Como and Cernobbio. The participation of commercial activities, schools, and associations creates a closer bond among the different actors in the community, promoting collaboration and the sharing of common values. The transformation of private spaces into collective works of art represents a tangible way to express pride in one's identity and history.

### Cultural and Touristic Impact
The initiative also has a significant impact on the cultural and touristic levels. The creation of a diffused garden along the streets and shores of the lake attracts the attention of visitors and tourists, contributing to the enhancement of the territory and the promotion of local tourism. Moreover, the participation of florists and local entities offers an opportunity to showcase local skills and creativity, reinforcing the image of Como and Cernobbio as cities at the forefront in the field of horticulture and floral design.

### Involvement of Schools and Associations
Schools and associations play a crucial role in this initiative. By involving the younger generations in the creation of floral compositions, 'Città in fiore' promotes environmental education and awareness of the importance of urban greenery. Associations, on the other hand, can use this occasion to organize events and activities that engage community members, creating a sense of belonging and active participation.

### Sustainability and Educational Value
The initiative also promotes sustainability and the educational value of urban greenery. The creation of diffused gardens contributes to improving air quality and creating green spaces accessible to everyone. Furthermore, the use of local plants and materials supports the local economy and promotes biodiversity. The initiative also offers an opportunity to educate citizens about the importance of caring for green spaces and environmental sustainability.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### How to Participate
To participate in 'Città in fiore', you need to fill out an online form with the materials of the setup created by Sunday, May 24, 2026. The form is available on the Orticolario website, where you can also find the complete rules and application procedures. The jury will select the most original balcony, which will be awarded with a spot on the official channels of Orticolario and Grandi Giardini Italiani.

### Tips for Creating Compositions
To create floral compositions that reflect the Tricolore, you can use local plants and materials. Florists and local entities involved in the initiative offer suggestions and materials to inspire participants. It is important to choose plants that are suitable for the local climate and require little maintenance to ensure the longevity of the compositions over time.

### Related Events and Activities
During the initiative period, related events and activities are planned to involve the community. Schools and associations can organize workshops and workshops to teach participants how to create floral compositions. Additionally, guided tours of the gardens and participating storefronts are planned to allow citizens to appreciate the beauty of the collective creations.

### Conclusion
'Città in fiore' represents a unique opportunity to celebrate the 80th anniversary of the Italian Republic and to promote green culture and sustainability. The active participation of the community, schools, and associations makes this initiative an example of how art and nature can come together to create a positive impact on the territory and society. For more information and to participate, visit the Orticolario website and discover how to contribute to this collective celebration.

[Find out more about how to participate in the 'Città in fiore' initiative](nav:calculator)

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/per-gli-80-anni-della-repubblica-orticolario-lancia-citta-in-fiore-sui-balconi-omaggiamo-tutti-il-tricolore/)*

item.title: Como company cuts CO2 with solar panels: 25 tons less
item.description: A photovoltaic system on an industrial roof in Cucciago reduces CO2 emissions by 25.71 tons per year, benefiting the local community.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- 99.55 kWp photovoltaic plant on 500 m² of industrial roof
- 119,460 kWh of clean energy per year
- 25.71 tons of CO2 less
- Initiative by Greenvolt Next Italia and Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli

## Key facts
- **What**: Installation of a photovoltaic plant on an industrial roof
- When: Project completed and operational
- Where: Cucciago plant, Como province
- Who: Greenvolt Next Italia and Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli
- Amount: 25.71 tons of CO2 saved annually
- Surface: 500 m² out of 1,300 m² total
- Power: 99.55 kWp
- Energy produced: 119,460 kWh/year

A photovoltaic plant that reduces emissions, improves the energy efficiency of the plant, and brings value to the local community. This is the heart of the new project carried out by Greenvolt Next Italia, part of the Greenvolt group, an international leader in the field of renewable energies, which supports Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli, a company founded in 1976, in its decarbonization path through the production of renewable energy in the Cucciago plant, in the province of Como. The intervention involved the refurbishment of the industrial roof over an area of approximately 500 square meters out of the 1,300 owned and the installation of a 99.55 kWp photovoltaic plant, already completed and fully operational. The system is capable of generating 119,460 kWh of clean energy per year, avoiding the emission of 25.71 tons of CO₂ every year, with a concrete contribution to reducing the environmental impact of the production site.

### Benefits for the community
The refurbishment of the industrial roof will improve the infrastructure efficiency and safety of the building. At the same time, the photovoltaic plant, consisting of 181 high-efficiency SunPower P7-550-COM modules, designed to maximize energy yield even on limited surfaces, will help reduce energy costs to support the production process, transforming the plant into a true asset for the production of renewable energy. The intervention will also strengthen the competitiveness of Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli in the supply of high-precision solutions for numerous industrial sectors: from hydraulics to medical instrumentation, from furniture to renewable energies, up to nautical, sectors in which reliability, quality, and production continuity represent strategic factors.

The project takes on even greater significance as the renewable plant will be made available to CER, CERS of Lario and Brianza, of which the municipality of Cucciago is a part, and the incentives generated by the sharing of energy will be reinvested in social initiatives for the local community. In this way, creating not only environmental, but also economic and social benefits for the community. - ## Implications for the Community and the Environment
The project carried out by Greenvolt Next Italia and Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli not only reduces CO2 emissions but also contributes to the well-being of the local community. The energy produced by the photovoltaic plant will be shared with CER, CERS of Lario and Brianza, allowing the reinvestment of incentives in social initiatives. This virtuous model strengthens the bond between companies, local authorities, and citizens, making renewable energy a concrete tool for territorial cohesion.

### Energy Transition and Competitiveness
For Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli, the intervention represents a further step towards a more efficient and sustainable production model. For Greenvolt Next Italia, it is confirmation of its ability to support companies in customized decarbonization projects, integrating infrastructure upgrading, renewable self-production, and innovative energy-sharing models. With over 800 projects completed in Italy and an installed capacity exceeding 200 MW, Greenvolt Next Italia is today one of the leading operators in the field of distributed renewable generation.

### Economic and Social Benefits
The photovoltaic plant not only reduces energy costs for the company but also contributes to creating shared value for the local community. The incentives generated by energy sharing will be reinvested in social initiatives, creating economic and social benefits for the community. This demonstrates how the energy transition can become a factor of shared development, capable of combining industrial competitiveness, environmental sustainability, and social responsibility.

### Examples of Similar Projects in Ticino
In Ticino, similar projects are emerging as models of sustainability and innovation. For example, the installation of solar panels on public and private buildings is becoming increasingly common, with benefits for both the environment and citizens' wallets. These projects not only reduce CO2 emissions but also contribute to job creation and stimulating the local economy.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do to start a similar project
If you are a company interested in reducing CO2 emissions and improving energy efficiency, you can follow some concrete steps to start a project similar to the one carried out by Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli and Greenvolt Next Italia.

### Step-by-step
1. **Initial assessment**: Contact an expert in renewable energies to evaluate the feasibility of a photovoltaic system on the roof of your company.
2. Design: Collaborate with a company specializing in the design and installation of photovoltaic systems to create a custom plan.
3. Installation: Follow the installation phases, ensuring that the system complies with local and national regulations.
4. Energy sharing: Evaluate the possibility of sharing the energy produced with the local community, benefiting from incentives and contributing to social well-being.
5. Monitoring and maintenance: Constantly monitor the performance of the system and plan periodic maintenance to ensure optimal operation.

### Useful tools
For more information and support, you can use the [solar energy calculator](nav:calculator) available on our website. This tool will help you estimate the energy potential of your roof and plan your project effectively.

### Conclusion
The project carried out by Benati Enrico Torneria Metalli and Greenvolt Next Italia represents a concrete example of how the energy transition can generate value beyond the company perimeter. Investing in renewable energies not only reduces CO2 emissions but also contributes to the well-being of the local community and the competitiveness of the company. By following the steps described, your company can also start a similar project and contribute to a more sustainable future.

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/il-tetto-dellazienda-comasca-diventa-un-super-impianto-fotovoltaico-25-tonnellate-di-co2-in-meno-allanno/)*

item.title: Ferrara (M5S): Beko in Cassinetta needs concrete results
item.description: After a year, the Five Star Movement asks for a check on the Beko plant in Cassinetta di Biandronno.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Ferrara (M5S) asks for concrete results after one year of Beko in Cassinetta
- Necessary verification to assess the impact of the plant
- Focus on employment and local development

## Key facts
- **What**: Verification of the Beko plant in Cassinetta di Biandronno
- When: After one year of activity, April 2024
- Where: Cassinetta di Biandronno, Varese
- Who: Five Star Movement, represented by Ferrara
- Amount: Initial investment of 50 million euros, with a promise to create 200 direct jobs and 500 indirect ones

Ferrara of the Five Star Movement has raised the issue of verifying the Beko plant in Cassinetta di Biandronno, emphasizing the need for concrete results after one year of activity. The request comes at a crucial moment for assessing the plant's impact on the territory, with a particular focus on employment and local development. Ferrara's initiative aims to ensure that the promises made at the start are kept, with special attention to the economic benefits for the local community.

### Implications for cross-border workers
The Beko plant in Cassinetta di Biandronno could have a significant impact on cross-border workers who commute to Ticino. With the increase in local job opportunities, many cross-border workers might consider moving or working in Italy, thus reducing the flow of workers towards the Canton of Ticino.
